title: Emergency Care: Cat with Limp Tail and Dragging Hind Legs
meta_desc: If your cat has a limp tail and is dragging its hind legs, seek emergency vet care immediately. Learn about saddle thrombus and spinal injuries.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden hind leg paralysis and a limp tail in cats are life-threatening emergencies often caused by blood clots or spinal trauma. Immediate veterinary intervention is required if your cat's legs are cold or they are in extreme pain.

Why has my cat suddenly lost the use of its back legs and tail?

When a cat suddenly loses the use of its hind legs and the tail becomes limp, it typically signifies a severe disruption in the neurological or vascular systems. This condition is often associated with Feline Aortic Thromboembolism (FATE), also known as a saddle thrombus, where a blood clot blocks blood flow to the lower extremities. It can also be caused by severe spinal trauma or a 'tail pull' injury that has damaged the nerves at the base of the spine.

Is sudden hind leg paralysis in cats a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a life-threatening medical emergency.
  • If your cat's back legs feel cold, the paw pads appear blue or pale, or your cat is vocalizing in extreme pain, you must transport them to an emergency veterinary hospital immediately.
  • Rapid intervention is the only way to manage pain and attempt to restore function.

How can taking a photo or video help the veterinary triage process?

  • Capturing a quick photo or video of your cat’s posture and how they are moving can be incredibly helpful for a triage team.
  • A photo of the paw pads can show the veterinarian the current status of blood circulation (cyanosis).
  • A video of the movement helps the clinical team distinguish between a neurological issue and a vascular blockage before you even arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Clinical signs, physical examination findings, and Doppler blood flow readings of the hindlimbs are essential for diagnosis. Doppler ultrasonography helps evaluate blood flow in the distal aorta and femoral arteries. Pathologic changes present distal to the level of the middle to lower thigh are characterized by focal muscle necrosis and degeneration of the central portions of the sciatic nerve and its branches. Misdiagnosis of neurologic abnormalities as an arterial thromboembolus is common. Analgesics, nursing care, and treatment of any underlying disease (e.g., cardiomyopathy) are important aspects of management. Neurologic deficits may improve within 2-3 weeks, but complete recovery may require 6 months, and permanent deficits are possible.

Chapter: Cardiology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 127)

faqs.question: What is a saddle thrombus?
faqs.answer: A saddle thrombus occurs when a blood clot, usually originating from the heart, travels through the aorta and lodges where the artery branches to the hind legs, cutting off all circulation.

faqs.question: Can my cat recover from hind leg paralysis?
faqs.answer: Recovery depends entirely on the underlying cause and how quickly treatment begins. While some cats regain mobility after intensive therapy, others may have permanent damage.

faqs.question: How can I keep my cat comfortable during transport?
faqs.answer: Place your cat in a secure carrier lined with soft towels. Avoid moving their spine or legs more than necessary, and get to the nearest emergency facility as quickly as possible.
